title: Probing charge noise in bilayer graphene quantum dots by Landau-Zener-Stückelberg-Majorana spectroscopy
authors: Katrin Hecker, Samuel Möller, Tobias Deußen, Hubert Dulisch, Luca Banszerus, Kenji Watanabe, Takashi Taniguchi, Christian Volk, Christoph Stampfer
abstract: Charge noise is an important factor limiting qubit coherence and relaxation in solid-state devices. In bilayer graphene (BLG) quantum dots, recently established as a promising platform for spin- and valley-based qubits, both the origin and magnitude of charge noise remain largely unexplored. Here, we investigate high-frequency charge noise using Landau-Zener-Stückelberg-Majorana (LZSM) interference spectroscopy. We study a single-particle charge qubit formed in a BLG double quantum dot at frequencies between 5 and 10 GHz and extract a noise spectral density $S_\varepsilon$ on the order of 0.5-0.9 neV$/\sqrt{\mathrm{Hz}}$. This is comparable to values reported for III-V semiconductor platforms and silicon. From the temperature and frequency dependence of the charge qubit decoherence, we conclude that thermal (Johnson) noise or electron-phonon coupling dominates over two-level fluctuators.
summary: This paper investigates high-frequency charge noise in bilayer graphene quantum dots using LZSM spectroscopy. It demonstrates that the noise levels are competitive with established semiconductor platforms and identifies thermal noise or electron-phonon coupling as the primary decoherence sources. This is significant for the development of graphene-based spin and valley qubits.
main_problem: The study aims to identify the microscopic origin and magnitude of high-frequency charge noise in bilayer graphene quantum dots, which is a limiting factor for qubit coherence.
main_result: The researchers found that the charge noise spectral density is comparable to silicon and III-V platforms and concluded that decoherence is driven by Johnson noise or electron-phonon coupling rather than two-level fluctuators.
method: The study utilizes Landau-Zener-Stückelberg-Majorana (LZSM) interference spectroscopy by applying a continuous microwave voltage signal to a double quantum dot.
model_or_system: A single-particle charge qubit formed in a bilayer graphene (BLG) double quantum dot encapsulated in hexagonal boron nitride (hBN) on a graphite gate, described by a two-level Hamiltonian with tunnel coupling and detuning.
key_observables: Current through the DQD, LZSM interference patterns, 2D Fourier transforms of interferograms, and noise spectral density (S_epsilon).
important_parameters: Driving frequency (5-10 GHz), temperature (80 mK to 1.2 K), interdot tunnel coupling (0.6-2.3 GHz), and noise spectral density (0.5-0.9 neV/sqrt(Hz)).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the Markovian limit of uncorrelated noise and a diabatic regime for the LZ transitions.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the device architecture and charge stability diagrams; Fig 2 displays the LZSM interference patterns and state evolution; Fig 3 shows temperature-dependent interference patterns and their Fourier transforms; Fig 4 presents the decay of Fourier amplitude and the temperature/frequency dependence of noise.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the device and platform, describes the LZSM spectroscopy technique, presents the experimental interference patterns, analyzes the temperature and frequency dependence of the noise, and concludes by ruling out specific decoherence mechanisms.
why_interesting: This paper is highly relevant to open quantum systems researchers as it provides an experimental characterization of a dissipative environment (noise spectral density) and identifies the specific physical mechanisms (Johnson noise vs. TLS) driving decoherence in a 2D material platform.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic illustration of the 2D heterostructure consisting of BLG encapsulated in hBN on a graphite gate. Cr/Au split gates (SGs) and two layers of finger gates (FGs) are deposited on top, separated by 15 nm of Al2O3. Voltages applied to the SGs and FGs define an n-type channel and a hole DQD (hDQD) with tunable interdot tunnel coupling ∆. A microwave signal with frequency f can be applied to the left FG via a bias-tee. (b) Charge stability diagram showing the logarithmic current I through the channel as a function of VR and VL at VSD = 700 µV. The white dashed lines mark the regime where an electron QD forms between the left and right FGs, while at lower gate voltages a hole...

FIG. 2. (a) LZSM interference pattern showing the current as a function of ε and the microwave amplitude A. The measurements were taken at a frequency of f = 6.6 GHz and a base temperature of T = 90 mK with Vm = −7.4 V and VSD = 1 mV. A line-by-line background has been subtracted from the current I to improve the visibility of the pattern; the resulting quantity is denoted throughout the manuscript by ˜I. (b) Schematic representations of the time evolution of the states E+ and E−(c.f. Fig. 1(d)) for different combinations of A and ε (I−III). While the total duration of one oscillation period T = 1/f, remains constant, the relative timing of the LZ transitions (t1, t2) and the phase...

FIG. 3. (a-c) LZSM interference patterns, as in Fig 2(a), measured at T = 80 mK (a), T = 500 mK (b) and T = 1 K (c), respectively. (d) Line cuts along the black dashed lines of the interference patterns. For a better visibility, an offset of multiples of 15 pA has been added. (e-g) 2D Fourier transformation of the current ˆIFT of the corresponding measurements shown in panels (a-c). The frequencies kA and kε have been normalized to the driving frequency f = 6.6 GHz. The blue dashed line in panel (e) marks one branch of the sinusoidal arc described by Eq. (4).

FIG. 4. (a) Logarithmic amplitude of the current ˆIFT along the sinusoidal arcs (shown in Figs. 3(e), 3(f) and 3(g)) as function of kε for three different temperatures. (b) Noise spectral density Sε as function of temperature T. (c) Sε as function of the applied frequency f. (d) Sε as function and the interdot coupling ∆, which is tuned by an appropriate voltage applied to the middle gate, as shown in Fig. 1(f).
